content.problemStatement:
There are nnn chocolates, and you are given an array of nnn numbers where the iii-th number AiA_iAi​ is the flavour type of the iii-th chocolate. Sebrina wants to eat as many different types of chocolates as possible, but she also has to save at least xxx number of chocolates for her little brother.

Find the maximum possible number of distinct flavour types Sebrina can have.


Input Format
The first line contains an integer TTT --- the number of test cases.



The first line of each test case consists of two integers nnn, xxx - The number of chocolates Sabrina has and the number of chocolates she has to save for her brother, respectively.
The second line contains nnn integers A1,…,AnA_1,\ldots, A_nA1​,…,An​, where the iii-th chocolate has type AiA_iAi​.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer denoting the maximum possible number of distinct chocolate flavours Sabrina can eat.
constraints: Sum of n over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T — the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers n and x — the total number of chocolates and the minimum number of chocolates Sabrina must save for her brother.
- The second line contains n integers A1 … An, where Ai denotes the flavour type of the i‑th chocolate.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line — the maximum possible number of distinct flavour types Sabrina can eat while keeping at least x chocolates for her brother.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: n=2, x=1 → she can eat at most 1 chocolate. There are 2 distinct flavours, so answer = min(2,1)=1.
Test case 2: n=4, x=2 → she can eat at most 2 chocolates, but all flavours are the same, distinct=1, answer=min(1,2)=1.
Test case 3: n=5, x=3 → she can eat at most 2 chocolates. Distinct flavours are {50,100}=2, answer=min(2,2)=2.
Distinct flavours = {1,2,3} → 3. She can eat at most 4 chocolates (6‑2). Answer = min(3,4)=3.
She must save all 5 chocolates, so she can eat 0. Distinct flavours are 5, but answer = min(5,0)=0.

solution.approach:
For each test case count the number of distinct flavour types present in the whole array (let it be D). Sabrina can eat at most n‑x chocolates, because she must keep at least x for her brother. To obtain D distinct flavours she would need at least D chocolates (one of each type). Therefore the maximum distinct flavours she can actually eat is the smaller of these two limits: answer = min(D, n‑x). The algorithm is:
1. Read n, x and the array.
2. Insert every Ai into a hash set (or sort and count) to obtain D.
3. Compute eatLimit = n - x.
4. Output min(D, eatLimit).
The set operation is O(1) average, so the total time per test case is O(n) and the memory usage is O(D) ≤ O(n).
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, where n is the number of chocolates in that test case. Overall O(Σ n) ≤ O(2·10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(D) ≤ O(n) for the set storing distinct flavours.
